pathways.label: General Sessions non-conviction expungement
pathways.summary: General Sessions non-convictions usually route through S.C. Code 17-1-40. The Judicial Branch says General Sessions charges under 17-1-40 are generally exempt from the Solicitor administrative fee, unless the dismissal, discharge, or nolle prosequi happened as part of a plea arrangement where the person pled guilty and was sentenced on other charges. ( "https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-general-sessions/") Wilma caution: If multiple charges came from one incident and some were dismissed while others resulted in a guilty plea, Wilma must ask whether the dismissal was part of the plea deal.…
ruleClauses: General Sessions non-convictions usually route through S.C. Code 17-1-40.

The Judicial Branch says General Sessions charges under 17-1-40 are generally exempt from the Solicitor administrative fee, unless the dismissal, discharge, or nolle prosequi happened as part of a plea arrangement where the person pled guilty and was sentenced on other charges. (
"https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-general-sessions/")

Wilma caution: If multiple charges came from one incident and some were dismissed while others resulted in a guilty plea, Wilma must ask whether the dismissal was part of the plea deal. That can affect fees and possibly how the packet is handled.
For magistrate or municipal court cases dismissed, nolle prossed, or resulting in not guilty, South Carolina has a more direct summary-court process under 17-22-950.

If the person was fingerprinted and the charge was dismissed, nolle prossed, or resulted in not guilty, the summary court generally issues the expungement order at no cost. However, the court should not issue the order if the dismissal happened at a preliminary hearing or if related charges from the same course of events are still pending in summary court or General Sessions. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

Prosecution or law enforcement may object within 30 days, but only on limited grounds: pending charges or an ineligible charge. If no objection is filed, the trial judge signs the order no sooner than 31 days and no later than 40 days after notice. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

If the person was not fingerprinted, the defendant applies at no cost using the summary-court application process, and the court issues the appropriate order if eligible. Internet public records must remove eligible criminal charges within 30 days after disposition, and the rule applies retroactively for older cases. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

"https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-general-sessions/") 4. Non-conviction expungement  General Sessions General Sessions non-convictions usually route through S.C. Code 17-1-40. Eligible outcomes include:

waitingRules: Prosecution or law enforcement may object within 30 days, but only on limited grounds: pending charges or an ineligible charge. If no objection is filed, the trial judge signs the order no sooner than 31 days and no later than 40 days after notice. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

If the person was not fingerprinted, the defendant applies at no cost using the summary-court application process, and the court issues the appropriate order if eligible. Internet public records must remove eligible criminal charges within 30 days after disposition, and the rule applies retroactively for older cases. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

A first offense conviction under South Carolinas fraudulent-check statute may be expunged after 1 year from conviction, but only if the conviction was a misdemeanor and the person had no other conviction during that one-year period. Felony fraudulent-check cases are not eligible under this route. This route is available only once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t34c011.php")

A person convicted of a crime carrying a penalty of not more than 30 days imprisonment or a $1,000 fine may apply after 3 years from the date of conviction, if they have no other convictions during that period. This route does not apply to offenses involving operation of a motor vehicle. The statute also now includes certain first offense unlawful-possession-of-firearm-or-weapon convictions with a penalty of not more than 1 year or a $1,000 fine. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

For third-degree domestic violence, the wait is 5 years from conviction, with no other conviction during the period. Conviction includes a guilty plea, nolo contendere plea, or bail forfeiture. Pending charges can block relief unless the pending charge is older than 5 years under the statutes tolling rules. This route is only available once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

A person sentenced under the South Carolina Youthful Offender Act may apply after completing the sentence, including probation and parole, and then remaining conviction-free for 5 years. Closely connected offenses from the same incident may count as one offense. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

A first offense conviction under the eligible failure-to-stop provision may be expunged after 3 years from completion of all sentence terms and conditions, if the person has no other conviction during that 3-year period. This route does not apply to felony failure-to-stop cases. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t56c005.php")
First offense failure to stop for blue light, non-felony route 56-5-750(F) 3 years after completing all sentence terms; no other convictions
Summary court internet public record removal Within 30 days of disposition
Failure to stop first offense, non-felony route 3 years after completing all sentence terms

If no objection is filed, the trial judge signs the order no sooner than 31 days and no later than 40 days after notice. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

AEP is generally for people who were at least 17 but under 21 at arrest, have no prior alcohol-related offense, and meet program criteria. Eligible offenses include several underage alcohol, open-container, public disorderly conduct, false-age, fake-ID, and related offenses, but DUI-type offenses are excluded. Successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition and can support expungement of official arrest records. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
AEP has a $250 program fee plus possible provider fees, but inability to pay cannot bar participation; the Solicitor may waive or reduce fees. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")

A first offense conviction under South Carolinas fraudulent-check statute may be expunged after 1 year from conviction, but only if the conviction was a misdemeanor and the person had no other conviction during that one-year period. Felony fraudulent-check cases are not eligible under this route. This route is available only once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t34c011.php")

Major exclusions include motor-vehicle offenses, violent crimes under 16-1-60, most Chapter 25 domestic-violence offenses except as specifically allowed, and sex-offender-registration offenses. A person who was merely eligible for YOA sentencing but was not sentenced under YOA is generally not eligible under this route, with a limited older-law exception. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

Juvenile expungement is governed by 63-19-2050. A person taken into custody, charged, or adjudicated delinquent for a status offense or nonviolent crime may petition to expunge official records. Violent-crime adjudications cannot be expunged under this route. The court must not grant expungement unless the person is at least 18, completed the disposition, has no subsequent adjudication or conviction, and has no pending Family Court or General Sessions charges. If the juvenile was found not guilty in Family Court, the court must grant expungement regardless of age and without a fee. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t63c019.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

pathways.label: Diversion or program-completion expungement
pathways.summary: After successful PTI completion, the Solicitor effects a noncriminal disposition, and the person may apply to destroy official arrest records. If the person violates PTI, prosecution resumes. PTI has a $100 application fee and $250 participation fee. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php") Alcohol Education Program - AEP AEP is generally for people who were at least 17 but under 21 at arrest, have no prior alcohol-related offense, and meet program criteria. Eligible offenses include several underage alcohol, open-container, public disorderly conduct, false-age, fake-ID, and related offenses, but DUI-type offenses are excluded.…

Alcohol Education Program - AEP

AEP is generally for people who were at least 17 but under 21 at arrest, have no prior alcohol-related offense, and meet program criteria. Eligible offenses include several underage alcohol, open-container, public disorderly conduct, false-age, fake-ID, and related offenses, but DUI-type offenses are excluded. Successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition and can support expungement of official arrest records. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
AEP has a $250 program fee plus possible provider fees, but inability to pay cannot bar participation; the Solicitor may waive or reduce fees. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
Traffic Education Program - TEP

TEP applies to qualifying traffic-related offenses punishable only by fine and loss of four points or less. It is not available if the offense resulted in death or serious bodily injury, and it is generally limited to people without a significant history of traffic violations. Successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
Conditional discharge - drug possession

Under 44-53-450, a person with no prior qualifying drug conviction who pleads or is found guilty of certain drug possession offenses may receive a conditional discharge. If they fulfill the terms, the court discharges the person and dismisses the proceeding without adjudication, and the person may apply to expunge official records. This route is one-time only. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t44c053.php")

The conditional-discharge fee before discharge/dismissal is $350 in General Sessions and $150 in summary court, with indigency-based waiver, reduction, or suspension possible. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t44c053.php")

ruleClauses: First offense misdemeanor fraudulent check - 34-11-90(e)

A first offense conviction under South Carolinas fraudulent-check statute may be expunged after 1 year from conviction, but only if the conviction was a misdemeanor and the person had no other conviction during that one-year period. Felony fraudulent-check cases are not eligible under this route. This route is available only once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t34c011.php")
First low-level conviction - 22-5-910

A person convicted of a crime carrying a penalty of not more than 30 days imprisonment or a $1,000 fine may apply after 3 years from the date of conviction, if they have no other convictions during that period. This route does not apply to offenses involving operation of a motor vehicle. The statute also now includes certain first offense unlawful-possession-of-firearm-or-weapon convictions with a penalty of not more than 1 year or a $1,000 fine. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

For third-degree domestic violence, the wait is 5 years from conviction, with no other conviction during the period. Conviction includes a guilty plea, nolo contendere plea, or bail forfeiture. Pending charges can block relief unless the pending charge is older than 5 years under the statutes tolling rules. This route is only available once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")
Youthful Offender Act - 22-5-920

A person sentenced under the South Carolina Youthful Offender Act may apply after completing the sentence, including probation and parole, and then remaining conviction-free for 5 years. Closely connected offenses from the same incident may count as one offense. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

Major exclusions include motor-vehicle offenses, violent crimes under 16-1-60, most Chapter 25 domestic-violence offenses except as specifically allowed, and sex-offender-registration offenses. A person who was merely eligible for YOA sentencing but was not sentenced under YOA is generally not eligible under this route, with a limited older-law exception. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")
Drug conviction route - 22-5-930

South Carolina has a separate route for certain first-offense drug convictions, including simple possession and some possession-with-intent categories. Wilma should treat this as manual-review unless the exact statute, offense level, conviction date, sentence completion date, and later conviction history are known. The Uniform Expungement Act expressly lists 22-5-930 as an expungement authorization for first offense simple possession or possession-with-intent drug convictions. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-carolina/title-17/chapter-22/section-17-22-910/")

Wilma caution: Do not route all drug convictions to 22-5-930. Confirm whether it was simple possession, possession of an unlawful prescription drug, first offense PWID, trafficking, distribution, manufacturing, or a different drug offense.
Failure to stop when signaled by law enforcement - 56-5-750(F)

A first offense conviction under the eligible failure-to-stop provision may be expunged after 3 years from completion of all sentence terms and conditions, if the person has no other conviction during that 3-year period. This route does not apply to felony failure-to-stop cases. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t56c005.php")

Wilma caution: South Carolina amended this statute, with certain changes effective May 12, 2026. Confirm the offense date, subsection, injury/death facts, high-speed pursuit facts, and felony/misdemeanor classification before routing. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t56c005.php")

South Carolina has a special route connected to old unlawful possession of handgun convictions under former 16-23-20 before the Constitutional Carry Act. The Judicial Branch SCCA 223C form references convictions before March 7, 2024, requires the application to be submitted before March 7, 2029, and limits the person to no prior application under 17-1-65. ("https://www.sccourts.org/media/forms/pdf/SCCA223C.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

The person must prove the connection by a preponderance of the evidence. ruleClauses: South Carolina allows trafficking survivors to seek relief when the offense was a direct result of, or interrelated to, trafficking victimization. A victim convicted or adjudicated delinquent of offenses under the trafficking article, prostitution, or another nonviolent misdemeanor or Class F felony may move the court to expunge the record if the offense was directly caused by or interrelated to trafficking. The person must prove the connection by a preponderance of the evidence. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t16c003.php")

For minors, the statute also protects trafficking victims under 18 from being prosecuted or adjudicated delinquent for certain trafficking-related/prostitution/nonviolent misdemeanor/Class F felony conduct if an investigation determines the conduct was caused by or interrelated to trafficking. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t16c003.php")

exclusionRules: Juvenile expungement is governed by 63-19-2050. A person taken into custody, charged, or adjudicated delinquent for a status offense or nonviolent crime may petition to expunge official records. Violent-crime adjudications cannot be expunged under this route. The court must not grant expungement unless the person is at least 18, completed the disposition, has no subsequent adjudication or conviction, and has no pending Family Court or General Sessions charges. If the juvenile was found not guilty in Family Court, the court must grant expungement regardless of age and without a fee. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t63c019.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

Prosecution or law enforcement may object within 30 days, but only on limited grounds: pending charges or an ineligible charge. If no objection is filed, the trial judge signs the order no sooner than 31 days and no later than 40 days after notice. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

AEP is generally for people who were at least 17 but under 21 at arrest, have no prior alcohol-related offense, and meet program criteria. Eligible offenses include several underage alcohol, open-container, public disorderly conduct, false-age, fake-ID, and related offenses, but DUI-type offenses are excluded. Successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition and can support expungement of official arrest records. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
AEP has a $250 program fee plus possible provider fees, but inability to pay cannot bar participation; the Solicitor may waive or reduce fees. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")

A first offense conviction under South Carolinas fraudulent-check statute may be expunged after 1 year from conviction, but only if the conviction was a misdemeanor and the person had no other conviction during that one-year period. Felony fraudulent-check cases are not eligible under this route. This route is available only once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t34c011.php")

Major exclusions include motor-vehicle offenses, violent crimes under 16-1-60, most Chapter 25 domestic-violence offenses except as specifically allowed, and sex-offender-registration offenses. A person who was merely eligible for YOA sentencing but was not sentenced under YOA is generally not eligible under this route, with a limited older-law exception. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

A first offense conviction under South Carolinas fraudulent-check statute may be expunged after 1 year from conviction, but only if the conviction was a misdemeanor and the person had no other conviction during that one-year period. Felony fraudulent-check cases are not eligible under this route. This route is available only once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t34c011.php")

A person convicted of a crime carrying a penalty of not more than 30 days imprisonment or a $1,000 fine may apply after 3 years from the date of conviction, if they have no other convictions during that period. This route does not apply to offenses involving operation of a motor vehicle. The statute also now includes certain first offense unlawful-possession-of-firearm-or-weapon convictions with a penalty of not more than 1 year or a $1,000 fine. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

For third-degree domestic violence, the wait is 5 years from conviction, with no other conviction during the period. Conviction includes a guilty plea, nolo contendere plea, or bail forfeiture. Pending charges can block relief unless the pending charge is older than 5 years under the statutes tolling rules. This route is only available once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

A person sentenced under the South Carolina Youthful Offender Act may apply after completing the sentence, including probation and parole, and then remaining conviction-free for 5 years. Closely connected offenses from the same incident may count as one offense. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")

A first offense conviction under the eligible failure-to-stop provision may be expunged after 3 years from completion of all sentence terms and conditions, if the person has no other conviction during that 3-year period. This route does not apply to felony failure-to-stop cases. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t56c005.php")

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Expungement
South Carolina expungement can remove the charge or conviction from public state records and direct agencies to destroy, seal, or remove records. But 17-1-40 expressly allows law enforcement and prosecutors to retain certain records under seal for limited periods or specific investigative/prosecution/litigation purposes. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c001.php")
Pardon
A South Carolina pardon is not the same as expungement. A pardon is state forgiveness and may restore certain rights, but it does not remove the conviction from the record by itself. South Carolinas pardon process generally runs through the Department of Probation, Parole and Pardon Services. ("https://sclegal.org/brochures/overcoming-a-criminal-record-expungement/")
Wilma rule: Do not tell a South Carolina user
a pardon clears your record.
Say:
A pardon may restore rights, but it is separate from expungement and does not automatically erase the conviction.

sourceSections.title: Official forms / offices Wilma should know
sourceSections.text:
South Carolina expungements usually start with either the Solicitors Office or the summary court, depending on the case.

Route Filing / form logic
General Sessions expungements Apply through the Solicitors Office in the circuit/county where the charge occurred
Magistrate / Municipal dismissed, nolle prossed, or not guilty charge Summary court uses Judicial Branch expungement forms, including SCCA 223-series forms
Summary court charge not fingerprinted Defendant applies at no cost using SCCA 223E; court issues expungement order
Summary court objection to expungement Prosecutor/law enforcement objection transmitted using SCCA 223D
Old unlawful handgun possession special route SCCA 223C order/form route for 17-1-65 cases
Juvenile expungement Family court / juvenile route under 63-19-2050
For General Sessions, the Solicitor sends the application to SLED to verify eligibility when required, obtains signatures if eligible, files the expungement order with the Clerk of Court, and provides certified copies to required agencies. (

"https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-general-sessions/")

sourceSections.title: Non-conviction expungement - General Sessions
sourceSections.text:
General Sessions non-convictions usually route through S.C. Code 17-1-40.
Eligible outcomes include:

Outcome Route
Charge dismissed 17-1-40
Nolle prosequi 17-1-40
Not guilty / acquittal 17-1-40
Discharged 17-1-40
The Judicial Branch says General Sessions charges under 17-1-40 are generally exempt from the Solicitor administrative fee, unless the dismissal, discharge, or nolle prosequi happened as part of a plea arrangement where the person pled guilty and was sentenced on other charges. (

"https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-general-sessions/")
Wilma caution: If multiple charges came from one incident and some were dismissed while others resulted in a guilty plea, Wilma must ask whether the dismissal was part of the plea deal. That can affect fees and possibly how the packet is handled.

sourceSections.title: Summary court non-conviction expungement
sourceSections.text:
For magistrate or municipal court cases dismissed, nolle prossed, or resulting in not guilty, South Carolina has a more direct summary-court process under 17-22-950.
If the person was fingerprinted and the charge was dismissed, nolle prossed, or resulted in not guilty, the summary court generally issues the expungement order at no cost. However, the court should not issue the order if the dismissal happened at a preliminary hearing or if related charges from the same course of events are still pending in summary court or General Sessions. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")
Prosecution or law enforcement may object within 30 days, but only on limited grounds: pending charges or an ineligible charge. If no objection is filed, the trial judge signs the order no sooner than 31 days and no later than 40 days after notice. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")
If the person was not fingerprinted, the defendant applies at no cost using the summary-court application process, and the court issues the appropriate order if eligible. Internet public records must remove eligible criminal charges within 30 days after disposition, and the rule applies retroactively for older cases. ("https://www.sccourts.org/resources/general-public/expungement-application-process/for-magistrate-municipal-courts/")

sourceSections.title: Diversion / program-completion routes
sourceSections.text:
Pretrial Intervention - PTI
After successful PTI completion, the Solicitor effects a noncriminal disposition, and the person may apply to destroy official arrest records. If the person violates PTI, prosecution resumes. PTI has a $100 application fee and $250 participation fee. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
Alcohol Education Program - AEP
AEP is generally for people who were at least 17 but under 21 at arrest, have no prior alcohol-related offense, and meet program criteria. Eligible offenses include several underage alcohol, open-container, public disorderly conduct, false-age, fake-ID, and related offenses, but DUI-type offenses are excluded. Successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition and can support expungement of official arrest records. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
AEP has a $250 program fee plus possible provider fees, but inability to pay cannot bar participation; the Solicitor may waive or reduce fees. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
Traffic Education Program - TEP
TEP applies to qualifying traffic-related offenses punishable only by fine and loss of four points or less. It is not available if the offense resulted in death or serious bodily injury, and it is generally limited to people without a significant history of traffic violations. Successful completion results in a noncriminal disposition. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t17c022.php")
Conditional discharge - drug possession
Under 44-53-450, a person with no prior qualifying drug conviction who pleads or is found guilty of certain drug possession offenses may receive a conditional discharge. If they fulfill the terms, the court discharges the person and dismisses the proceeding without adjudication, and the person may apply to expunge official records. This route is one-time only. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t44c053.php")
The conditional-discharge fee before discharge/dismissal is $350 in General Sessions and $150 in summary court, with indigency-based waiver, reduction, or suspension possible. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t44c053.php")

sourceSections.title: Conviction expungement routes
sourceSections.text:
First offense misdemeanor fraudulent check - 34-11-90(e)
A first offense conviction under South Carolinas fraudulent-check statute may be expunged after 1 year from conviction, but only if the conviction was a misdemeanor and the person had no other conviction during that one-year period. Felony fraudulent-check cases are not eligible under this route. This route is available only once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t34c011.php")
First low-level conviction - 22-5-910
A person convicted of a crime carrying a penalty of not more than 30 days imprisonment or a $1,000 fine may apply after 3 years from the date of conviction, if they have no other convictions during that period. This route does not apply to offenses involving operation of a motor vehicle. The statute also now includes certain first offense unlawful-possession-of-firearm-or-weapon convictions with a penalty of not more than 1 year or a $1,000 fine. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")
For third-degree domestic violence, the wait is 5 years from conviction, with no other conviction during the period. Conviction includes a guilty plea, nolo contendere plea, or bail forfeiture. Pending charges can block relief unless the pending charge is older than 5 years under the statutes tolling rules. This route is only available once. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")
Youthful Offender Act - 22-5-920
A person sentenced under the South Carolina Youthful Offender Act may apply after completing the sentence, including probation and parole, and then remaining conviction-free for 5 years. Closely connected offenses from the same incident may count as one offense. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")
Major exclusions include motor-vehicle offenses, violent crimes under 16-1-60, most Chapter 25 domestic-violence offenses except as specifically allowed, and sex-offender-registration offenses. A person who was merely eligible for YOA sentencing but was not sentenced under YOA is generally not eligible under this route, with a limited older-law exception. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t22c005.php")
Drug conviction route - 22-5-930
South Carolina has a separate route for certain first-offense drug convictions, including simple possession and some possession-with-intent categories. Wilma should treat this as manual-review unless the exact statute, offense level, conviction date, sentence completion date, and later conviction history are known. The Uniform Expungement Act expressly lists 22-5-930 as an expungement authorization for first offense simple possession or possession-with-intent drug convictions. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/south-carolina/title-17/chapter-22/section-17-22-910/")
Wilma caution: Do not route all drug convictions to 22-5-930. Confirm whether it was simple possession, possession of an unlawful prescription drug, first offense PWID, trafficking, distribution, manufacturing, or a different drug offense.
Failure to stop when signaled by law enforcement - 56-5-750(F)
A first offense conviction under the eligible failure-to-stop provision may be expunged after 3 years from completion of all sentence terms and conditions, if the person has no other conviction during that 3-year period. This route does not apply to felony failure-to-stop cases. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t56c005.php")
Wilma caution: South Carolina amended this statute, with certain changes effective May 12, 2026. Confirm the offense date, subsection, injury/death facts, high-speed pursuit facts, and felony/misdemeanor classification before routing. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t56c005.php")
Old unlawful handgun possession - 17-1-65 / SCCA 223C
South Carolina has a special route connected to old unlawful possession of handgun convictions under former 16-23-20 before the Constitutional Carry Act. The Judicial Branch SCCA 223C form references convictions before March 7, 2024, requires the application to be submitted before March 7, 2029, and limits the person to no prior application under 17-1-65. ("https://www.sccourts.org/media/forms/pdf/SCCA223C.pdf?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

sourceSections.title: Human trafficking survivor expungement
sourceSections.text:
South Carolina allows trafficking survivors to seek relief when the offense was a direct result of, or interrelated to, trafficking victimization. A victim convicted or adjudicated delinquent of offenses under the trafficking article, prostitution, or another nonviolent misdemeanor or Class F felony may move the court to expunge the record if the offense was directly caused by or interrelated to trafficking. The person must prove the connection by a preponderance of the evidence. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t16c003.php")
For minors, the statute also protects trafficking victims under 18 from being prosecuted or adjudicated delinquent for certain trafficking-related/prostitution/nonviolent misdemeanor/Class F felony conduct if an investigation determines the conduct was caused by or interrelated to trafficking. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t16c003.php")
Wilma trauma rule: Ask only what is needed to route:

Was this charge connected to trafficking, coercion, exploitation, survival circumstances, or someone forcing or directing you to do something connected to the case?

Do not require graphic details. Trigger manual/legal review.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile expungement
sourceSections.text:
Juvenile expungement is governed by 63-19-2050. A person taken into custody, charged, or adjudicated delinquent for a status offense or nonviolent crime may petition to expunge official records. Violent-crime adjudications cannot be expunged under this route. The court must not grant expungement unless the person is at least 18, completed the disposition, has no subsequent adjudication or conviction, and has no pending Family Court or General Sessions charges. If the juvenile was found not guilty in Family Court, the court must grant expungement regardless of age and without a fee. ("https://www.scstatehouse.gov/code/t63c019.php?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma caution: Juvenile status offense is treated more favorably than nonviolent delinquency adjudication. Ask whether the juvenile matter was a status offense, nonviolent offense, violent offense, dismissal, not guilty, or adjudication.

sourceSections.text:

PTI After successful completion

Juvenile status/nonviolent offense Usually after age 18, completed disposition, no later adjudication/conviction, no pending charges

sourceSections.title: Record request process
Wilma intake decision tree
Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For South Carolina, train Wilma with this core logic:
South Carolina is expungement-friendly only in narrow statutory lanes.Start with court level: summary court, General Sessions, or Family Court.Non-convictions are usually the cleanest route.Program completions - PTI, AEP, TEP, conditional discharge - are major routes.Conviction expungement depends on the exact statute: fraudulent check, 22-5-910 low-level conviction, DV third, YOA, drug offense, failure to stop, old handgun possession, trafficking survivor, or juvenile.Most routes require no later convictions and no pending charges.Do not promise expungement until Wilma has the exact charge, final disposition, court, sentence completion date, prior/later conviction history, and pending-charge status.
